questions.stem: In a coding interview, you are asked: In a min-heap, where is the smallest element stored? Which response is most accurate?
options: At the root (index 0 or 1 depending on convention)
At the last index of the array
questions.explanation: A min-heap maintains the heap property that every parent is ≤ its children, so the global minimum is at the root.

questions.stem: For a binary heap stored in a 0-indexed array, what is the left child index of node i?
questions.explanation: In 0-indexed layout: left = 2i+1, right = 2i+2, parent = (i-1)//2.

questions.stem: For a binary heap stored in a 0-indexed array, what is the parent index of node i (i > 0)?
questions.explanation: Parent of i is floor((i-1)/2) in 0-indexed heaps.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, you are asked: What is the height of a complete binary heap with n nodes? Which response is most accurate?
questions.explanation: A complete binary tree of n nodes has height floor(log₂ n).

questions.stem: In a coding interview, you are asked: How does insert typically work in a binary heap? Which response is most accurate?
options: Insert at root and sift down always
Place at next leaf position, then sift/bubble up
Append unsorted; rebuild whole tree each time
Insert into a sorted array position via binary search
questions.explanation: New element goes at the end to keep completeness, then bubble-up restores the heap property.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, you are asked: What does extract-min on a min-heap typically do? Which response is most accurate?
options: Swap root with last element, remove last, then sift down from root
Remove an arbitrary leaf and rebuild
Scan the array for the min each time
questions.explanation: Root holds the min; after removing it you move the last leaf to root and heapify down.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, you are asked: Time complexity of insert and extract-min in a binary heap? Which response is most accurate?
questions.explanation: Height is O(log n); sift-up/sift-down walk at most that far.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, you are asked: What does heapify (sift-down) do starting at index i? Which response is most accurate?
options: Converts a BST into a heap
Restores heap property in the subtree rooted at i assuming children are heaps
questions.explanation: Classic heapify assumes left/right subtrees are already heaps and fixes the root of that subtree.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, you are asked: Building a heap from an unordered array with Floyd's bottom-up heapify is:? Which answer is best?
questions.explanation: Bottom-up heap construction is linear: most nodes are near the leaves so sift costs sum to O(n).

questions.stem: In a coding interview, how should you answer this: A max-heap differs from a min-heap primarily in that:? Choose the strongest option.
options: The array must be sorted descending
questions.explanation: Max-heap property: parent ≥ children; min-heap: parent ≤ children.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, how should you answer this: In Java, PriorityQueue by default is a:? Choose the strongest option.
questions.explanation: java.util.PriorityQueue is a min-heap under Comparable/natural order unless a reverse Comparator is supplied.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, how should you answer this: peek() on a PriorityQueue (min-heap) returns:? Choose the strongest option.
options: The current minimum without removing it
questions.explanation: peek/top inspects the root without deletion.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, you are asked: Which structure is typically NOT a heap? Which response is most accurate?
options: AVL tree guaranteeing sorted inorder traversal
questions.explanation: AVL is a balanced BST, not a heap; heaps do not keep a total inorder order.

questions.stem: Why are heaps often preferred over sorted arrays for a dynamic priority queue?
options: Insert and extract are both O(log n) without shifting the whole array
Heaps support O(1) arbitrary delete by value always
Heaps use less memory than one integer
questions.explanation: Maintaining a fully sorted array makes inserts O(n); heaps balance insert/extract at O(log n).

questions.stem: In a coding interview, how should you answer this: In a 1-indexed heap array, the right child of i is:? Choose the strongest option.
questions.explanation: 1-indexed convention: left=2i, right=2i+1, parent=i//2.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, how should you answer this: A complete binary tree (as used by binary heaps) means:? Choose the strongest option.
options: Every node has 0 or 2 children only
It is always a perfect tree of 2^k-1 nodes
All levels full except possibly the last, which is filled left to right
Leaves may appear on any level arbitrarily
questions.explanation: Completeness enables the compact array representation without gaps.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, how should you answer this: Finding the minimum in a max-heap of n distinct elements is:? Choose the strongest option.
options: Θ(n) in the worst case (scan leaves / all nodes)
questions.explanation: Max-heap root is the max; the min is among the leaves and requires a linear scan in general.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, how should you answer this: decrease-key in a binary heap (when you have the index) is typically:? Choose the strongest option.
options: O(log n) via sift-up after lowering the key
questions.explanation: Lowering a key may violate parent order; bubble up restores it in O(log n).

questions.stem: In a coding interview, you are asked: Which operation is NOT efficiently supported by a standard binary heap? Which answer is best?
options: Search for an arbitrary value in O(log n)
questions.explanation: Heaps are not ordered for search; finding an arbitrary key is O(n) unless you keep an index map.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, how should you answer this: Compared to a balanced BST priority queue, a binary heap usually:? Choose the strongest option.
options: Always supports O(log n) ordered traversal of all keys
Gives O(1) delete-by-value without extra maps
Uses less pointer overhead and has better cache locality in an array
Stores keys in sorted order in the array
questions.explanation: Array heaps are compact; BSTs pay pointer overhead but offer ordered iteration and easier delete-by-handle.

questions.stem: Top-K largest elements from a stream using a heap of size K typically uses a:
options: Sorted array of all elements always
Min-heap of size K storing the current top-K
questions.explanation: A min-heap of K keeps the smallest of the top-K at the root; replace when a larger candidate arrives.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, you are asked: Why use a min-heap of size K for top-K largest (not a max-heap of size K)? Which answer is best?
options: You need O(1) access to the smallest among candidates to decide eviction
Min-heap stores the largest at the root
questions.explanation: Eviction decision compares against the weakest (smallest) member of the current top-K.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, how should you answer this: Time to find top-K from n elements with a size-K heap is:? Choose the strongest option.
questions.explanation: Each of n elements may cause an O(log K) heap operation.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, how should you answer this: The two-heap median trick keeps:? Choose the strongest option.
options: A sorted list and a heap
Max-heap for lower half, min-heap for upper half
questions.explanation: Lower half's max and upper half's min sit at the two roots so the median is O(1) to read.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, how should you answer this: After inserting into the two-heap median structure, you must:? Choose the strongest option.
options: Move all elements to the min-heap
Rebalance so heap sizes differ by at most one
questions.explanation: Size balance keeps the median at one root or the average of both roots.

questions.stem: In Dijkstra with a binary heap priority queue, decrease-key / re-insert is used because:
options: Tentative distances improve and priorities must update
questions.explanation: Relaxation can improve d[v]; the PQ must reflect the new better priority (via decrease-key or lazy duplicate entries).

questions.stem: Lazy Dijkstra (insert duplicates instead of decrease-key) is correct if you:
options: Ignore stale entries when a worse distance is popped
Delete the graph after first pop
questions.explanation: When popping, skip if the distance is not the current best known for that node.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, how should you answer this: Heap sort's main idea is:? Choose the strongest option.
options: Partition like quicksort around a pivot
Build a heap then repeatedly extract-max/min into the sorted region
questions.explanation: Heap sort = build-heap + n extract operations; in-place variant swaps root with end.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, how should you answer this: Worst-case time of heap sort is:? Choose the strongest option.
questions.explanation: Build is O(n); n extracts cost O(n log n) total.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, how should you answer this: A d-ary heap with larger d tends to:? Choose the strongest option.
options: Always dominate binary heaps on every operation
Make decrease-key/sift-up cheaper (shallower) but sift-down more expensive
Remove the need for the heap property
questions.explanation: Height ~ log_d n shrinks up-sifts; down-sifts scan d children so they cost more.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, how should you answer this: k-way merge of sorted lists with a heap uses the heap to:? Choose the strongest option.
options: Always pull the next smallest head among k list heads
Binary search each list fully each time
questions.explanation: Classic pattern: push each list's front; pop min and push that list's next.

questions.stem: For 'Kth largest in an unsorted array', a size-K min-heap approach returns the answer as:
options: The root of the min-heap after processing all elements
questions.explanation: After processing, the min-heap of K largest has the Kth largest at its root.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, how should you answer this: Priority queues in OS schedulers often use heaps because:? Choose the strongest option.
options: They efficiently fetch the next highest-priority ready task
questions.explanation: Extract-max/min matches 'run highest priority next' efficiently.

questions.stem: If you need both insert and delete-arbitrary-by-id fast, a pure binary heap is awkward because:
options: You need an index/handle map from id → heap position
questions.explanation: Without knowing the index, finding the node is O(n); production PQ libraries expose handles.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, you are asked: When is a sorted list better than a heap as a priority queue? Which response is most accurate?
options: When n is tiny or you need frequent full ordered scans
questions.explanation: For tiny sets, constants dominate; sorted structures also help if you iterate all keys in order often.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, how should you answer this: Meldable heaps (e.g., pairing/Fibonacci) shine when you need:? Choose the strongest option.
options: Efficient merge of two priority queues
questions.explanation: Binary heaps don't meld cheaply; some advanced heaps support fast union.

questions.stem: In an interview, 'reorganize string so no two identical letters are adjacent' often uses a heap to:
options: Always place the currently most frequent remaining character (with a cool-down)
questions.explanation: Max-heap by frequency greedily schedules the next letter; cool-down queue holds the last used.

questions.stem: Replace elements with next greater element using a monotonic stack differs from a heap approach mainly in:
options: Stack exploits left-to-right adjacency structure; heap is for priority by value globally
questions.explanation: Next-greater is positional; heaps don't encode 'next to the right' without extra work.

questions.stem: Capacity planning: a min-heap of meeting end times helps room assignment by:
options: Checking whether the earliest-ending room is free for the next start
questions.explanation: Classic 'minimum meeting rooms' / allocation: pop heap if end ≤ start, else need a new room.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, how should you answer this: Compared to quickselect for Kth element, a heap-of-K approach:? Choose the strongest option.
options: Is simpler and O(n log K); quickselect expected O(n) but harder to get right
questions.explanation: Interviewers accept heap-of-K for clarity; quickselect is faster expected time but careful coding.

questions.stem: You implement Dijkstra with a binary heap on a dense graph (E ≈ V²). The bottleneck is often:
options: O((V+E) log V) with many PQ ops — dense graphs may prefer other structures/algorithms
questions.explanation: Binary-heap Dijkstra is O((V+E) log V); on dense graphs Fibonacci/heaps or other algos may matter, and Floyd is sometimes simpler for all-pairs.

questions.stem: Why can 'insert duplicate nodes' Dijkstra use more memory than decrease-key?
options: The graph stores copies of all edges in the heap
Each improvement pushes another PQ entry, so the queue can hold O(E) entries
questions.explanation: Lazy Dijkstra trades decrease-key complexity for potentially many stale heap entries.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, you are asked: A senior bug: median of a stream drifts after deletions. Likely cause? Which answer is best?
options: Heaps not rebalanced / lazy deletion not cleaning both heaps consistently
Min-heap used for both halves intentionally
questions.explanation: Two-heap median with deletions needs careful lazy delete + rebalance invariants.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, you are asked: When would you pick a 4-ary heap over binary for Dijkstra in practice? Which answer is best?
options: Shallower tree can improve decrease-key-heavy workloads and cache behavior
questions.explanation: Higher arity reduces height; some contest/library Dijkstra implementations use 4-heaps.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, how should you answer this: Prove briefly why bottom-up build-heap is O(n): the key insight is:? Choose the strongest option.
options: Nodes at height h cost O(h) and there are ≤ n/2^{h+1} of them, summing to O(n)
questions.explanation: The geometric series over heights yields a linear bound — classic CLRS argument.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, you are asked: Priority queue stability (equal priorities): binary heaps are typically:? Which answer is best?
options: FIFO stable by default like a queue
Unstable unless you add a tie-breaker (insertion counter)
Required to be unstable by the JVM
questions.explanation: Equal keys may extract in any order; add sequence numbers for stable scheduling.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, how should you answer this: In soft real-time systems, a risk of heap-based schedulers is:? Choose the strongest option.
options: O(log n) extract jitter under load; design must bound queue size / use constant-time structures if needed
Priority inversion is solved by heaps alone
questions.explanation: Logs grow with ready-queue size; hard RT often wants tighter bounds or hierarchical queues.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, how should you answer this: Indexable PQ (heap + map) delete(id) steps are:? Choose the strongest option.
options: Swap with last, pop, then sift up/down from the hole; update map indices
Set key to infinity and ignore forever without fixing structure
questions.explanation: Handle-based delete must restore heap shape and keep the position map coherent.

questions.stem: All-pairs shortest paths: repeatedly running heap-Dijkstra from each source is preferred over Floyd-Warshall when:
options: Graph is sparse and non-negative weights — O(V(E+V) log V) can beat O(V³)
questions.explanation: Sparse non-negative: V×Dijkstra wins; dense or negatives change the calculus.

questions.stem: Interview follow-up: convert BST to min-heap in-place discussion — the hard constraint is:
options: Heap needs complete shape + heap order; BST inorder is sorted so you need a careful rebuild or extra array
questions.explanation: Different invariants; usually extract sorted keys then build-heap, or tree-to-array techniques.
